threats.title: mintYieldFee Missing Access Control Allows Theft of Accrued Yield Fees
threats.hypothesis_description: The mintYieldFee function is external with no access control modifier. Any caller can mint accrued yield fee shares (_yieldFeeTotalSupply) to any recipient address, stealing yield fees intended for the _yieldFeeRecipient. The function only checks vault collateralization and that _shares <= _yieldFeeTotalSupply, but never validates that the caller or recipient is authorized.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct missing access control vulnerability. The _yieldFeeRecipient state variable exists but is never consulted in mintYieldFee, creating a straightforward theft vector for any on-chain actor.

proof_of_signal.pattern: External function mintYieldFee(uint256 _shares, address _recipient) has no onlyOwner or access control modifier; _yieldFeeRecipient is never checked against _recipient

preconditions: _yieldFeeTotalSupply > 0 (yield fees have been accrued via liquidation)
Vault is collateralized (_currentExchangeRate() >= _assetUnit)
_yieldFeePercentage > 0 (fees are being collected during liquidation)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: mintYieldFee is callable by any address and mints to any _recipient without checking _yieldFeeRecipient
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - no admin check exists on this function

operational_questions: Is there an external wrapper contract that restricts access to mintYieldFee?
How frequently does _yieldFeeTotalSupply accumulate to meaningful values?
validation_requirements: Confirm no inherited modifier or external access restriction
Test calling mintYieldFee from an unauthorized address

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor Vault for _yieldFeeTotalSupply > 0 by calling yieldFeeTotalSupply()
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Receive Vault shares representing the entire accrued yield fee
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Redeem shares for underlying assets via withdraw/redeem

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete theft of all accrued yield fees
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Yield fee recipient receives nothing; vault economics disrupted
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Unrecoverable - shares minted and potentially redeemed
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Yield fee distribution, vault economic model

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Monitor mintYieldFee calls from addresses != yieldFeeRecipient
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Rapid mint followed by redeem from unknown address

threats.title: sponsor() Griefing Attack Revokes Victim Prize Eligibility
threats.hypothesis_description: The Vault.sponsor() function deposits a tiny amount of assets for a target _receiver and then calls _twabController.sponsor(_receiver), which overwrites the _receiver's delegation to SPONSORSHIP_ADDRESS. This removes the victim's entire delegateBalance (prize eligibility) regardless of how large their existing balance is. Any attacker can call sponsor(1, victimAddress) to revoke any user's prize eligibility at negligible cost.
threats.investigation_rationale: The sponsor function unconditionally delegates the receiver's balance to SPONSORSHIP_ADDRESS, affecting the receiver's ENTIRE existing balance, not just the sponsored deposit. This is a clear access control violation where one user can modify another user's prize eligibility.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _sponsor deposits for _receiver then calls _twabController.sponsor(_receiver) which delegates _receiver's ENTIRE balance to SPONSORSHIP_ADDRESS, overriding any existing delegation

preconditions: Victim has a non-zero balance in the Vault
Victim is not already delegating to SPONSORSHIP_ADDRESS
Attacker has a tiny amount of the underlying asset to deposit

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: sponsor() allows any caller to force _receiver's delegation to SPONSORSHIP_ADDRESS
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Persistent - any time a user has a balance in the vault

operational_questions: Can the victim recover by calling twabController.delegate(vault, self)?
Can the attacker repeatedly grief the same victim?
What is the minimum deposit amount needed (1 wei of underlying)?
validation_requirements: Test sponsor(1, victim) when victim has existing balance and delegation
Verify victim's delegateBalance goes to 0 after attack
Check if victim's TWAB history is corrupted

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify high-value depositors in a Vault
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Approve 1 wei of underlying asset to the Vault
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Victim's delegateBalance drops to 0, losing prize eligibility for current draw

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Any user's prize eligibility can be revoked at will
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Targeted users lose prizes; can be repeated continuously
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Recoverable via twabController.delegate(), but victim must detect and act

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Before draw close for maximum impact

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: sponsor() calls with minimal _assets and _receiver != msg.sender
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Multiple sponsor calls targeting different receivers from same caller

threats.title: Vault _deposit Allows Free Share Minting When Idle Assets Exist
threats.hypothesis_description: The Vault._deposit function checks the vault's underlying asset balance and skips transferring from the caller when the vault holds sufficient assets (_assets <= _vaultAssets). If _assets > _vaultAssets but vault holds some assets, only the difference is transferred. This allows a depositor to receive shares backed by the vault's pre-existing idle assets without paying for them, effectively stealing value from existing shareholders.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _deposit function conflates vault idle balances with depositor payments. Any underlying assets sitting in the vault (from direct transfers, incomplete yield vault sweeps, or unit mismatch in liquidation flow) can be claimed by the next depositor.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (_assets > _vaultAssets) only transfers difference; if _assets <= _vaultAssets skips transfer entirely. Depositor gets shares for full _assets amount while paying less or nothing.

preconditions: Vault holds non-zero underlying asset balance (e.g., from direct ERC20 transfer to vault address)
Vault is collateralized (deposits enabled)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _deposit uses vault balance to subsidize new deposits instead of always pulling full amount from caller
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time vault holds idle underlying assets

operational_questions: Under what conditions do assets accumulate in the vault?
Does the liquidation flow's unit mismatch (OPS-H005) create exploitable idle assets?
Are there other sources of idle assets (airdrops, refunds, rounding)?
validation_requirements: Send underlying assets directly to vault, then deposit with another account
Verify depositor receives shares without paying equivalent assets
Test the liquidation sweep condition with different exchange rates

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor vault's underlying asset balance via IERC20(asset).balanceOf(vault)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Receive shares without transferring any assets
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Redeem shares for underlying assets from yield vault
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - timing-dependent on assets being present

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Unrecoverable - shares minted and potentially redeemed

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Opportunistic - when vault has idle assets

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Deposits where transferred amount < minted shares value
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Rapid deposit after assets appear in vault

threats.title: Vault liquidate() Unit Mismatch Leaves Assets Stranded for Theft
threats.hypothesis_description: In Vault.liquidate(), the sweep condition compares _amountOut (in vault shares) with _vaultAssets (in underlying asset units): `if (_vaultAssets != 0 && _amountOut >= _vaultAssets)`. When the exchange rate is not 1:1, this comparison is invalid. When exchange rate > 1 asset per share, shares are worth more than their numeric value in assets, causing the sweep to not trigger even when it should. Stranded assets can then be stolen via the _deposit vulnerability (OPS-H003).
threats.investigation_rationale: The comparison mixes incompatible units. This is a prerequisite for the idle asset theft in OPS-H003, as it provides a mechanism for assets to accumulate in the vault during normal protocol operations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _amountOut (shares) compared directly with _vaultAssets (underlying asset balance) without conversion through exchange rate

preconditions: Exchange rate != 1:1 (diverges over time as deposits/withdrawals occur)
Vault holds some underlying assets at time of liquidation
_amountOut in shares is numerically less than _vaultAssets in asset units

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Direct numeric comparison of shares vs assets in sweep condition
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever exchange rate diverges from 1:1 and vault holds idle assets

operational_questions: How often does the exchange rate deviate from 1:1?
Under what conditions do underlying assets sit in the vault?
What is the typical _amountOut during liquidation?
validation_requirements: Deploy vault with non-1:1 exchange rate
Execute liquidation when vault holds assets
Verify sweep condition fails due to unit mismatch

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for exchange rate to deviate from 1:1
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Send small amount of underlying asset to vault directly
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Assets remain in vault, exploit via OPS-H003
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: May require seeding the vault with assets

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Assets stranded in vault available for theft
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Assets recoverable only if swept before exploitation

threats.title: Canary Tier Claim Bypasses Expansion Threshold Causing Premature Tier Expansion
threats.hypothesis_description: In _computeNextNumberOfTiers, _nextNumberOfTiers is initialized to largestTierClaimed + 2. When the canary tier (numberOfTiers - 1) is claimed, _nextNumberOfTiers becomes numberOfTiers + 1, which triggers tier expansion BEFORE the claimExpansionThreshold conditions are evaluated. The threshold check cannot reduce _nextNumberOfTiers, only increase it, so a single canary claim forces expansion regardless of whether sufficient claims were made to warrant it.
threats.investigation_rationale: Canary tiers are designed to be easy to win (high odds), so a single canary claim is likely each draw. If this alone triggers expansion, the tier system expands prematurely, diluting prize liquidity across more tiers and reducing individual prize sizes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _nextNumberOfTiers = largestTierClaimed + 2 sets expansion before threshold check; threshold check only sets _nextNumberOfTiers = _numTiers + 1 which is same or less than already-set value

preconditions: Canary tier is claimed during the draw (largestTierClaimed = numberOfTiers - 1)
numberOfTiers < MAXIMUM_NUMBER_OF_TIERS (room for expansion)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: largestTierClaimed + 2 == numberOfTiers + 1 when canary is claimed, bypassing threshold
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every draw where canary tier is claimed

operational_questions: Is the canary tier expected to be claimed every draw?
Does tier expansion without threshold checks match design intent?
What is the economic impact of premature expansion?
validation_requirements: Claim only canary tier with low claimCount, verify expansion occurs
Compare numberOfTiers before and after closeDraw with single canary claim

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for draw close and claim only the canary tier prize
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Observe tier expansion on next closeDraw call
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Repeat to force system to maximum tiers, diluting all prizes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Profit from reduced competition in higher tiers or grief the protocol
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - natural behavior or minimal effort

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Prize liquidity spread too thin, smaller prizes than intended
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Tier contraction occurs naturally but slowly

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Rapid tier expansion without proportional claim growth

threats.title: contributePrizeTokens Attribution Theft via Delta Balance Race
threats.hypothesis_description: PrizePool.contributePrizeTokens is permissionless and allows any caller to attribute prize tokens (already transferred to the pool) to any vault address. The function validates against the delta balance (unaccounted tokens) but does not verify that the caller actually sent the tokens. If tokens are transferred to the PrizePool and the intended vault's contributePrizeTokens call is delayed (even by one block), another actor can call contributePrizeTokens for their own vault, stealing the contribution attribution.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function uses a delta-balance pattern without sender verification. On L2s without public mempools, this requires multi-block observation rather than front-running, but the code vulnerability is real.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Delta balance check (balanceOf - _accountedBalance) allows any caller to attribute unaccounted tokens to any _prizeVault address; no verification that caller transferred the tokens

preconditions: Prize tokens transferred to PrizePool but not yet attributed (delta > 0)
Attacker observes the unattributed balance on-chain
Attacker calls contributePrizeTokens before the intended vault does

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Anyone can call contributePrizeTokens for any vault with any unaccounted balance
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between token transfer to PrizePool and contributePrizeTokens call

operational_questions: Is the token transfer + contributePrizeTokens always atomic in the liquidation flow?
Can the increaseReserve function create exploitable delta balance?
Are there cases where tokens sit unattributed between blocks?
validation_requirements: Trace the full liquidation flow to verify atomicity
Test calling contributePrizeTokens from an unauthorized address when delta > 0

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor PrizePool for unaccounted token balance (balanceOf - accountedBalance > 0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call contributePrizeTokens(attackerVault, deltaAmount) to steal attribution
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker vault's users gain increased vault portion for prize claims
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Legitimate vault's contribution call fails with ContributionGTDeltaBalance
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Timing-dependent - must observe and act between blocks

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Legitimate vaults lose prize allocation; attacker vault gains unfairly
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Contribution attribution is permanent once recorded

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: contributePrizeTokens calls from addresses other than the contributing vault
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Delta balance consumed by unexpected caller
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Rapid contributePrizeTokens calls immediately after token transfers

threats.title: Malicious VaultHooks Grief Batch Prize Claims via Revert
threats.hypothesis_description: Winners can set arbitrary hook implementations via setHooks(). The Vault._claimPrize function makes external calls to hooks.implementation.beforeClaimPrize() and afterClaimPrize() without try/catch. If a winner's hook reverts, the entire batch claim transaction from the Claimer reverts, preventing all other winners in the batch from receiving their prizes and wasting the claimer's gas.
threats.investigation_rationale: The hook calls are not wrapped in error handling. A single malicious hook in a batch of claims poisons the entire transaction. Claimers must then identify and exclude the malicious user, costing gas and delaying other legitimate claims.

proof_of_signal.pattern: External calls to hooks.implementation.beforeClaimPrize and afterClaimPrize without try/catch; revert propagates to batch caller

preconditions: Winner sets hooks with useBeforeClaimPrize=true or useAfterClaimPrize=true
Hook implementation always reverts
Claimer includes this winner in a batch claim

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: External hook calls not wrapped in try/catch; batch claims are all-or-nothing
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - user sets their own hooks
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every draw where the malicious winner has a claimable prize

operational_questions: Can claimers detect malicious hooks before submitting batch claims?
What is the gas cost to the claimer for failed batch transactions?
Can a malicious hook consume excessive gas (gas griefing)?
validation_requirements: Set reverting hook, include in batch claim, verify entire batch fails
Measure gas cost of failed batch

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deposit into vault to become eligible for prizes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call setHooks with a hook implementation that always reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Win prizes (especially canary tier with high odds)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Every claimer batch including this winner reverts

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: No admin compromise, but claim pipeline disrupted
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Claimers waste gas, other winners' claims delayed
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Claimers must manually exclude malicious users from batches

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: setHooks calls deploying unusual hook implementations

threats.title: yieldFeePercentage at FEE_PRECISION (100%) Causes Division by Zero in liquidate
threats.hypothesis_description: The _setYieldFeePercentage function uses a strict greater-than check (>) instead of greater-than-or-equal (>=), allowing yieldFeePercentage to be set to exactly FEE_PRECISION (1e9, representing 100%). In liquidate(), the yield fee calculation performs division by (FEE_PRECISION - _yieldFeePercentage), which becomes division by zero when fee is 100%, permanently bricking the liquidation mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a boundary condition bug in the validation logic. While it requires the owner to set this value, even an accidental misconfiguration permanently disables the core yield-to-prize conversion mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (yieldFeePercentage_ > FEE_PRECISION) uses > instead of >=; liquidate line ~462: (FEE_PRECISION - _yieldFeePercentage) = 0 when fee = FEE_PRECISION

preconditions: Vault owner calls setYieldFeePercentage(1e9)
Subsequent liquidation attempts

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Off-by-one in validation allows 100% fee, causing division by zero
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires onlyOwner to set the fee
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent once set - liquidation permanently broken

operational_questions: Can the owner recover by setting a lower fee percentage?
Does the vault have any other mechanism to convert yield to prizes?
validation_requirements: Set yieldFeePercentage to FEE_PRECISION
Attempt liquidation and confirm revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: All subsequent liquidation calls revert with division by zero
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Vault stops generating prizes, yield accumulates unused
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users lose prize eligibility from this vault permanently

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Vault permanently unable to convert yield to prizes
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete halt of prize generation from this vault
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Recoverable if owner sets fee back to < 1e9

threats.title: setLiquidationPair Grants Unnecessary Max Asset Approval Enabling LP Drain
threats.hypothesis_description: When setLiquidationPair is called, it approves type(uint256).max of the underlying asset to the new LiquidationPair. The liquidation flow only involves minting vault shares and contributing prize tokens - the LP should never need to pull underlying assets. A malicious or vulnerable LP contract can call asset.transferFrom(vault, attacker, balance) to drain the vault's underlying assets, bypassing the intended yield-only liquidation flow.
threats.investigation_rationale: The approval is unnecessary for the LP's role and creates an excessive trust surface. If the LP contract has any vulnerability or if the owner maliciously sets a rogue LP, all underlying assets in transit through the vault are at risk.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _asset.safeApprove(address(liquidationPair_), type(uint256).max) grants LP unlimited access to vault's underlying assets, which LP never needs for its liquidation role

preconditions: Owner sets a malicious/vulnerable LiquidationPair
Vault holds underlying assets (even transiently during deposits)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: LP receives max approval for underlying asset but only needs to contribute prize tokens
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Persistent once malicious LP is set

operational_questions: Does the LP ever need to pull underlying assets from the vault?
What assets sit in the vault at any given time?
Is the LP contract audited and immutable?
validation_requirements: Review LP contract for transferFrom calls targeting vault's underlying asset
Verify LP only needs prize token interaction, not underlying asset access

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy malicious LP contract with transferFrom backdoor
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Malicious LP calls asset.transferFrom(vault, attacker, vault_balance)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All underlying assets drained from vault

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete drain of vault's underlying assets
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Vault becomes undercollateralized, all users lose funds

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: LP contract calling transferFrom on vault's underlying asset
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected asset outflows from vault to LP

threats.title: PrizePool closeDraw Truncates Contributions via uint96 Cast Causing Silent Fund Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: In PrizePool.closeDraw(), the contribution amount for the draw is cast to uint96: `_nextDraw(_nextNumberOfTiers, uint96(_contributionsForDraw(lastClosedDrawId + 1)))`. The _contributionsForDraw function returns uint256. If contributions exceed type(uint96).max (~79.2 billion with 18 decimals), the truncation silently discards the excess. These truncated tokens remain in the PrizePool but are never distributed as prizes or reserve, becoming permanently stranded.
threats.investigation_rationale: The truncation happens silently without revert, causing permanent loss of contributed prize tokens. While 79.2 billion tokens is large, with high-value tokens or aggressive contribution strategies, this ceiling could be reached.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint96(_contributionsForDraw(lastClosedDrawId + 1)) silently truncates uint256 to uint96, losing any value above type(uint96).max

preconditions: Total contributions for a single draw exceed type(uint96).max (~79.2B with 18 decimals)
Draw is closed via closeDraw()

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unsafe downcast from uint256 to uint96 without overflow check
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - occurs during normal draw closure
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When total draw contributions exceed uint96 max

operational_questions: What are the expected maximum contribution levels per draw?
Could low-decimal tokens (e.g., USDC with 6 decimals) be used as prize tokens?
With 6-decimal tokens, uint96 max is ~79 trillion - is this realistic?
validation_requirements: Contribute amount > type(uint96).max and close draw
Verify excess tokens are stranded in PrizePool

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Contribute massive prize token amount in single draw
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Draw is closed, contributions truncated to uint96
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Excess tokens remain in PrizePool, permanently unaccounted
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Protocol solvency unaffected but tokens lost to contributors

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Contributed tokens permanently lost from distribution
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Tokens cannot be recovered - stuck in PrizePool
